_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Algorithm Problems: Part 2
summary: Track more than one piece of state and handle edge cases first.
refresher: A multi-step algorithm may remember a best value and where it appeared. Write the rule that should remain true after every loop pass, decide what happens for empty input and ties, then trace a small example.

blocks.body: A harder algorithm often remembers several related facts. To find the position of the largest value, remember the best position found so far and compare every later value with it. Decide the empty-input result before reading position zero.

A rule that stays true after every loop pass is called an invariant. Here the rule is simple: after checking through position `i`, `best` stores the position of the largest value seen so far. Writing that sentence first makes the update easier to verify.

blocks.title: The algorithm is not C++-specific
blocks.body: You would keep the same best-so-far rule in Python. C++ adds explicit vector and index types, but the reasoning and the small hand trace are identical.

blocks.caption: The empty case is handled before position zero is used. Each larger value replaces the saved best position.
Handle empty input before creating an element-based candidate.

blocks.body: For `{4, 9, 7}`, `best` starts at zero, changes to one after nine, and stays one after seven. Using `>` keeps the first position when two maximum values tie. Using `>=` would keep the last tied position.

blocks.title: Handle empty input before choosing a candidate
blocks.body: Position zero is a valid starting candidate only when the vector contains an element. Check `.empty()` before reading or comparing any element.

blocks.why: The first element already supplies the current candidate, so only later positions need comparison.
An equal later value does not satisfy greater-than, preserving the earlier index.
